food_subgroup: Chinese noodle soups / Yunnan rice noodle dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Crossing the Bridge Noodles (Guo Qiao Mi Xian). Mapped to the nearest parent class 'noodle soup food product' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Yunnan-style rice noodle soup. A0FKP (Soups and broths) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Chinese regional dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Crossing the Bridge Noodles is approximately 600 g, comprising a large bowl of bone broth with a full complement of raw ingredients and a separate portion of pre-cooked rice noodles assembled tableside.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken noodle soup, homemade-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with rice noodle and mixed broth component data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Crossing the Bridge Noodles (Guo Qiao Mi Xian) exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are estimated by component analogy: rice noodle soup base (USDA FDC #172945 — soup, chicken noodle, homemade), adjusted for higher protein content from mixed meats and bone broth, and moderate fat from the surface oil layer. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.2 g per 100 g based on mixed animal-fat broth composition. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available at this resolution and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate applied to Crossing the Bridge Noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Crossing the Bridge Noodles as a composite dish. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~53–61 range for rice noodles). The presence of significant protein (4.5 g/100 g) and fat (3.5 g/100 g) from bone broth and mixed meats attenuates the glycemic response relative to plain rice noodles alone. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 57.0) / 100 = 31.4. Carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 9.5 × 6 = 57.0 g. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low = 0–55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. The input record labelled GI level as 'low' at GI 55, which is consistent with the University of Sydney boundary definition.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bone broth (280 mg/100 g; approximately 1,680 mg per 600 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid consuming the broth, request reduced-salt preparation, and restrict portion size, as a full serving may contribute up to 73% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~31 per serving) from rice noodles, partially attenuated by co-ingested protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor rice noodle portion size, as the glycemic load per full serving is moderate; pairing with the protein-rich broth and lean meat toppings helps blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and saturated fat from bone broth and animal-fat surface oil layer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit broth consumption to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake; choosing lean meat toppings (chicken breast, pork loin) and requesting less oil can meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk from this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crossing the Bridge Noodles (过桥米线, Guòqiáo mǐxiàn) is the most celebrated dish of Yunnan Province in Southwest China. It is a distinctive interactive soup experience in which a large bowl of intensely hot, rich bone broth — made from slow-simmered chicken and pork bones and sealed with a thin layer of oil to retain heat — is served alongside an array of raw accompaniments: paper-thin slices of pork, chicken, or liver; fresh leafy vegetables; mushrooms; tofu skin; a raw quail egg; and a separate bowl of pre-cooked fresh rice noodles (mi xian). The diner assembles the dish tableside by adding the raw ingredients in sequence — meats first to poach instantly in the boiling broth, then vegetables, then noodles — creating a personalised, freshly cooked meal. The flavour profile is clean, deeply savoury, and comforting, with a silky broth base and tender, lightly poached proteins. Texturally, the rice noodles are smooth and slippery, contrasting with the soft vegetables and delicate meat slices. Nutritionally, a standard 600 g serving provides approximately 510 kcal, 27 g protein, 21 g fat, and 57 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high (~1,680 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate (~31 per serving), and the protein-fat matrix of the broth attenuates the glycemic response from the rice noodles. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is named after a legend in which a scholar's devoted wife crossed a bridge daily to deliver hot soup, discovering that a layer of oil preserved the broth's heat. It is a staple of Yunnan cuisine and widely available across China and in Chinese restaurants internationally.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Crossing the Bridge Noodles exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by component analogy from USDA FoodData Central chicken noodle soup and rice noodle data, cross-checked against the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney rice noodle data. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite Yunnan-style rice noodle soup are not available at the required resolution in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of April 2026. 2. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from mixed animal-fat bone broth composition using USDA FDC component data; not directly measured. 3. gi_category is set to 'low': a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney GI Database classification (low = 0–55; medium = 56–69; high = 70+). The input record labelled GI level as 'low' at GI 55, which is consistent with this boundary definition. Previous iteration incorrectly set gi_category to 'medium'; corrected in this iteration. 4. Duplicate portion entry in input data (two identical '1 serving (600g)' entries) — deduplicated to a single 600 g serving. 5. Input source 'Yunnan Academy of Agricultural Sciences' is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central used instead. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 7. Halal status set to false as pork is a primary ingredient.
